skill.name: ubs
skill.description: Ultimate Bug Scanner helps AI coding workflows run pre-commit static analysis across multiple languages, with guidance for staged scans, CI output, and agent hooks.
skill.summary: Run fast static analysis checks before commits and CI merges.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: The skill is documentation-only, and most static findings are false positives from Markdown examples that mention risky patterns or show normal CLI usage. The material concern is the installation guidance at SKILL.md:264, which pipes a remote GitHub script directly into bash and creates real supply-chain execution risk for users.

high_findings.title: Remote Script Piped Directly to Shell
high_findings.description: The installation section recommends fetching an installer from a hardcoded GitHub URL and piping it directly into bash. This is not proof of malicious intent, but it asks users to execute remote code without inspection, pinning, or integrity verification.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line explicitly combines a network download with shell execution. The context is an installation command for users, so the supply-chain risk is real even if the source may be legitimate.

medium_findings.title: Manual System Path Installation Command
medium_findings.description: The manual installation example downloads an executable from a hardcoded URL into /usr/local/bin and marks it executable. This is a legitimate install pattern, but it still executes unverified remote content with elevated filesystem impact.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command writes a remote artifact into a system binary path and changes permissions. It is presented as documentation, so the risk depends on user execution rather than skill runtime behavior.

medium_findings.title: Agent Hook and Git Hook Command Execution Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents shell hook patterns for Claude Code and Git pre-commit hooks that execute UBS during file writes or commits. These are expected for the tool, but marketplace users should understand that enabling hooks runs local commands automatically.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The snippets are clearly hook examples and not hidden behavior. Automatic local command execution is still security-relevant because hooks can affect developer workflows.

low_findings.title: Risky Code Terms Used as Scanner Examples
low_findings.description: References to eval(), send(), innerHTML, unsafe blocks, and similar patterns describe what UBS detects. These are not executable code paths inside the skill and appear to be false positives from documentation tables and examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The terms appear in Markdown tables and illustrative suppression examples. I found no evidence that the skill itself evaluates dynamic code.

low_findings.title: Hidden Configuration Path Documentation
low_findings.description: The hidden file and directory references are normal user configuration paths for custom UBS rules and agent integrations. They do not indicate covert access by the skill file itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are disclosed in documentation and are used to show user-managed rule and hook locations. No evidence found of hidden persistence or unauthorized file reads in the skill content.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Examples Triggered Static Rules
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are documented CLI examples for running UBS, scoping scans, producing reports, and troubleshooting. They are relevant usage instructions, not concealed commands executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible Markdown usage examples and align with the stated scanner purpose. The main exception is the remote shell installation command, which is reported separately at high severity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: A hardcoded GitHub installer URL is downloaded with curl and piped to bash. Users cannot easily verify the script before execution from this one-line command.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This exact pattern is present in the installation section and is a well-known supply-chain hazard. It is not hidden, but it is still dangerous guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution in Example Scan Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The staged-file example uses shell command substitution to pass Git output into UBS. It is ordinary shell usage, but filenames with unusual characters can make this pattern less robust than a built-in staged mode.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution exists and is user-facing, but the same section provides a cleaner --staged alternative. Risk is limited to users copying the shell example.

content.value_statement: AI-assisted changes can introduce subtle bugs before review. UBS gives developers a fast pre-commit scanner with CI formats and agent hook guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to run UBS on specific files, staged changes, diffs, or full projects.
Documents output formats including text, JSON, JSONL, SARIF, and HTML reports.
Lists detection categories for null safety, security, async issues, memory leaks, and code quality.
Shows CI, Git pre-commit, and Claude Code hook integration patterns.
Describes custom AST rule locations and command-line category filtering.
Provides troubleshooting commands for environment and dependency checks.
limitations: The skill is documentation and does not include the scanner binary itself.
Users must install UBS and its dependencies outside the skill.
The recommended one-line installer executes remote shell code and needs review before use.
Scanner accuracy depends on language support, project configuration, and rule quality.

use_cases.title: Pre-Commit Quality Gate
use_cases.description: Run UBS on staged or changed files before committing AI-generated code.

use_cases.title: CI Regression Checks
use_cases.description: Generate strict CI results and compare new findings against a saved baseline.

use_cases.title: Agent Hook Setup
use_cases.description: Connect scanner commands to coding-agent hooks so file changes trigger checks.

prompt_templates.title: Scan My Changed Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the UBS guidance to scan my staged files. Explain any findings in plain language and suggest the safest fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Pre-Commit Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add a Git pre-commit workflow for UBS. Keep the hook simple and explain when it should block a commit.

prompt_templates.title: Configure CI Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a UBS CI command for this repository that fails on warnings and produces a machine-readable report for review.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Rules and Baselines
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a rollout plan for UBS using language filters, category selection, custom rules, and baseline comparison for this project.

output_examples.input: A developer asks whether to commit AI-generated TypeScript changes.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends scanning staged files, reviewing each reported location, fixing critical issues first, and rerunning the scan before commit.

output_examples.input: A team wants scanner results in GitHub code scanning.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests a CI command that runs in strict mode and emits SARIF for GitHub ingestion.

output_examples.input: A project has too many findings during initial rollout.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends scoping scans to changed files, using a baseline, and tuning categories before enforcing all warnings.

best_practices: Review installer scripts before running commands that pipe network content to a shell.
Start with changed-file scans, then expand enforcement after teams understand the findings.
Treat suppressions as documented exceptions and rerun UBS after each fix.
anti_patterns: Do not run remote installation commands blindly in production environments.
Do not suppress scanner findings without verifying the root cause.
Do not rely only on full project scans when a staged-file workflow is faster and clearer.

faq.question: What does this skill help me do?
faq.answer: It explains how to use UBS as a static analysis gate for AI-assisted coding workflows.

faq.question: Does the skill include the UBS executable?
faq.answer: No. It provides usage guidance and installation examples for the external UBS tool.

faq.question: Is the one-line installer safe to run?
faq.answer: It should be reviewed first because it downloads remote content and pipes it to bash.

faq.question: Can UBS run in CI?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation shows CI mode, strict warning handling, and report formats such as JSON and SARIF.

faq.question: Which tools are mentioned for agent integration?
faq.answer: The skill mentions Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Gemini, Windsurf, and Cline hook or rule locations.

faq.question: What are the main limits of UBS?
faq.answer: It can produce false positives, requires local setup, and depends on supported languages and configured rules.
